coredata.dc:title: Synergetic effect of the combination of Prosopis Farcta extract with sodium dodecyl sulfate on corrosion inhibition of St37 steel in 1M HCl medium
coredata.dc:creator: Heragh, Morteza Ferdosi
Tavakoli, Hossein
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the inhibition effect of Prosopis farcta (PF) extract combined with Sodium Dodecyl Sulfate (SDS) on the corrosion of St37 steel in 1 M HCl was investigated by electrochemical tests (polarization and EIS) and surface examination (AFM and FESEM). The results of polarization tests showed that the critical micelle concentration (CMC) of SDS in 1 M HCl at ambient temperature is 400 mg/L. It was found that the combination of 800 mg/L PF extract and 400 mg/L SDS have a synergistic effect. The highest inhibition effect for St37 steel in 1 M HCl was 90%, which was achieved when 800 mg/L PF was combined with 400 mg/L SDS. The adsorption of this inhibitor follows the Langmuir isotherm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synergetic effect
Prosopis farcta extract
Corrosion inhibitor
SDS

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bonds of Prosopis farcta and SDS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) molecular structure of SDS (b) Schematic diagram of the electrochemical cell used in the research.
The change of open circuit potential (Eocp) and Potentiodynamic diagram for St37 steel in 1 M HCl in the presence of different concentrations of A) PF B) SDS.
Variations in corrosion inhibition of different concentrations of SDS and PF extract for St37 steel in 1 M HCl at ambient temperature according to the potentiodynamic polarization test.
Results and fitted EIS data in the presence of 400 mg/lit of SDS and different concentrations of PF extract: A) Nyquist plot B) Bode plot C) Phase diagram.
Potentiodynamic diagram for St37 steel in 1 M HCl in the presence of a) 100 mg/L b) 200 mg/L c) 400 mg/L and d) 800 mg/L of PF extract along with A) 100 mg/L B) 200 mg/L, and C) 400 mg/L of SDS.
Results of AFM analysis for St37 steel exposed to 1 M HCL with different concentrations of SDS and PF.
Average surface roughness in the presence of different concentrations of PF alongside 400 mg/L SDS.

FESEM images taken from the surface of St37 steel specimens immersed in 1 M HCl in the presence of and a) 400 mg/L of SDS and 0 mg/L of PF extract, b) 400 mg/L of SDS and 100 mg/L of PF extract, c) 400 mg/L of SDS and 200 mg/L of PF extract, d) 400 mg/L of SDS and 400 mg/L of PF extract, , e) 400 mg/L of SDS and 800 mg/L of PF extract and f) blank.
Langmuir adsorption isotherm plot for A) PF and B) SDS in 1 M HCl.
Chemical composition of St.37 steel used in the research.
Data extracted by extrapolation from Tafel polarization curve for St37 steel in 1 M HCl in the presence of different concentrations of PF extract.
Data extracted by extrapolation from Tafel polarization curve for St37 steel in 1 M HCl in the presence of different concentrations of SDS.
Parameters extracted from the EIS test for the corrosion of St37 steel in 1 M HCl in the presence of different concentrations of PF extract with 400 mg/L SDS.
Data extracted from the polarization test for SDS + PF.

head.ce:title: Synergetic effect of the combination of Prosopis Farcta extract with sodium dodecyl sulfate on corrosion inhibition of St37 steel in 1M HCl medium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials and Metallurgical Engineering, Semnan University, Semnan, P.O. Box: 35131-19111, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials and Metallurgical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials and Metallurgical Engineering, Semnan University, Semnan, P.O. Box: 35131-19111, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the inhibition effect of Prosopis farcta (PF) extract combined with Sodium Dodecyl Sulfate (SDS) on the corrosion of St37 steel in 1 M HCl was investigated by electrochemical tests (polarization and EIS) and surface examination (AFM and FESEM). The results of polarization tests showed that the critical micelle concentration (CMC) of SDS in 1 M HCl at ambient temperature is 400 mg/L. It was found that the combination of 800 mg/L PF extract and 400 mg/L SDS have a synergistic effect. The highest inhibition effect for St37 steel in 1 M HCl was 90%, which was achieved when 800 mg/L PF was combined with 400 mg/L SDS. The adsorption of this inhibitor follows the Langmuir isotherm.

ce:section.ce:para: Given the fast pace of industrial development and the essential role of metals and alloys in almost all industrial activities, the performance of these materials including their resistance against corrosion has long been a popular subject of research. There are various methods to prevent corrosion in metal components, one of which is to use corrosion inhibitors [1]. Although corrosion inhibitors play a key role in preventing metal corrosion in industrial settings, unfortunately, many common corrosion inhibitors are toxic substances that can cause substantial environmental damage [2]. Hence, the possibility of finding environment-friendly compounds for metal corrosion inhibition has attracted the attention of many researchers.

One of the most important parameters affecting the corrosion inhibition property of a compound is its adsorption on the metal surface. Heteroatoms such as N, S, O or P play an essential role in the adsorption of inhibitors in acidic environments, and many studies on corrosion inhibitors have reported an improved absorption in the presence of these elements [3-6]. These elements have high electron density which allows them to act as corrosion inhibitors. These elements create a series of adsorption sites on the metal surface, which improve the adsorption of the inhibitor onto the electrode surface. The inhibition power of these elements can be described as O

The tannin compounds in some plants, there is the role of corrosion inhibitor. Tannin compounds are also present in the structure of Prosopis farcta, which can be found in abundance in the deserts of Semnan (Iran). Given the presence of tannin in Prosopis farcta, it may be possible to use the extract of this plant as a corrosion inhibitor [15].There are several groups and varieties of corrosion inhibitors, one of which is surfactants. Because of their high adsorption on metal surfaces, surfactant-containing inhibitors exhibit a very high inhibition performance. In other words, it has been shown that the presence of surfactants alongside inhibitors improves the adsorption of the inhibitory agent on the electrode surface [16] Surfactant adsorption on metal surfaces has a significant impact on the corrosion resistance of these surfaces [17] and is, therefore, a subject worthy of much attention. The mechanism of inhibitory impact of cationic compounds on corrosion of steel in acidic environments is not yet well understood [18, 19].

Following the previous study of the authors, which investigated -for the first time- the corrosion inhibition effect of Prosopis farcta extract [15], the present study explored the effect of adding an anionic surfactant called Sodium Dodecyl Sulfate (SDS) to Prosopis farcta (PF) extract on corrosion inhibition. For this purpose, the synergetic/antagonistic impact of using SDS alongside PF extract on corrosion inhibition for steel in 1 M hydrochloric acid was investigated.
The metal used in this study was St.37 steel with the chemical composition given in Table 1 .

Corrosion tests were carried out in 1 M HCl manufactured by Merck. This electrolyte was prepared at ambient temperature and then mixed with certain amounts of PF extract and SDS to create solutions of specific concentrations. PF extract was prepared using a method previously developed by the authors [15]. The molecular structure of SDS is shown in Fig. 1 -a.

To create test specimens for corrosion tests, sheets of size 20×10×1 mm3 were cut from a slab of St37 steel, sanded with 80 to 2500 grit sandpapers, and finally polished by a special polishing cloth with 0.3 µm alumina powder for 10 min. After this surface preparation, specimens were cleaned by acetone, rinsed with water, and air-dried.

Electrochemical tests were performed in a flat cell manufactured by EG&G. The schematic diagram of this cell is illustrated in Fig. 1-b. In this cell, a 1cm2 area of the specimen surface is exposed to the electrolyte. Initially, the specimens were immersed in the electrolyte for 30 min to allow the open-circuit potential (OCP) to stabilize. The potentiodynamic tests and the Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) tests were carried out with an Origa Flex OGF500 potentiostat. The reference electrode was SCE (Saturated Calomel Electrode) and the auxiliary electrode was platinum. In the Tafel polarization test, the applied potential was changed from near −750 mV to near −150 mV, the scanning rate was 1 mV/s, and the corrosion current density was determined by the extrapolation of Tafel lines. In the EIS test, the frequency was changed from 100 kHz to 10mHz and potential was changed in the range of ±10 mV relative to OCP.

Using the current density obtained from the Tafel test, the inhibition efficiency was calculated by Equation 1:
(1)
% I E =
i 0
−
i
i n h .
i 0
× 100
where
i 0
is the corrosion current without the inhibitor and
i
i n h .
is the corrosion current in the presence of the inhibitor.

The following equation was used to determine the inhibition efficiency based on the polarization resistance in the EIS test:
(2)
% I E =
R
c t
(
i n h .
)
−
R
c t .
R
c t
(
i n h .
)
× 100
where
R
c t .
is the polarization resistance without the inhibitor and
R
c t (
i n h .
)
is the polarization resistance in the presence of the inhibitor.

Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) was used to examine the surface of the specimens and observe the effect of PF+SDS on the surface corrosion. This microscopic examination of the specimens provided FESEM images showing the existence and severity of surface corrosion. For this examination, sanded and polished St37 steel specimens were immersed in the electrolytes with different PF/SDS compositions and also in the electrolyte without these compounds for 24 h, and then FESEM images were taken by a TESCAN Mira 3 FESEM system.

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) was used to examine the topography of the specimens and the corrosion inhibition effect of PF extract on the steel surface. For this analysis, perfectly sanded and polished specimens were immersed in the electrolytes with different PF/SDS compositions and without these compounds for 8 h. This analysis was performed because the inhibition effect of PF extract and SDS can be examined in terms of the smoothness/roughness of the treated surface and AFM allows researchers to study the surface roughness at the nanoscale. This examination was performed using a Veeco-TT AFM system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bonds of Prosopis farcta and SDS
ce:section.ce:para: In a previous study on the corrosion inhibition effect of PF extract [15], the results of FTIR analysis showed that this extract has several bonds that may produce the inhibition effect. In that study, the main absorbance was observed at 3424.82cm−1 representing a strong NH bond. Also, a peak observed at 1622.99cm−1 was related to NH group bonds and a peak at 2925.28 cm−1 was attributed to aliphatic CH bonds. The peaks that appeared at 1730.30cm−1 were attributed to the carbonyl groups in esters (R-COO-R) and aldehydes (R-COH). The peak observed at 1520.65cm−1 was related to the C = N bond or the aromatic C = C rings. A peak at 1451.53cm−1 was attributed to the CC bond system, and the peaks at 1375.20cm−1 and 1239cm−1 were related to the presence of Aryl-OH. Also, the peaks observed at 1033cm−1 indicated the presence of CN bonds. From these results, it was concluded that PF extract contains a mixture of oily compounds, alkaloids, and flavonoids.

According to studies carried out by Lun et al. [20], in SDS, the peaks at 1188cm−1 represent the S = O group [21], those at 1468 cm−1 represent the CN group [22], and the peaks at 2834cm−1~3007cm−1 represent the CH group [23]. Thus, it is clear that SDS contains S, N, and O related bonds that explain why it is so easily adsorbed on the metal surface.

For the potentiodynamic polarization analysis, the inhibition effects of PF extract and SDS surfactant at different concentrations (mg/L) on corrosion in 1 M HCl were tested. The results of the potentiodynamic test and the change of open circuit potential (Eocp) are presented in Fig. 2 .

The kinetic parameters of corrosion reactions, which were extracted from Tafel extrapolation, are presented in Table 2 . According to Table 2, it can be seen that the addition of PF and SDS to the electrolyte (1 M HCl) has affected both Tafel reactions (cathodic and anodic), such as hydrogen reduction and metal dissolution. The reduced corrosion current has decreased the corrosion rate of St37 steel in 1 M HCl medium and prevented cathodic and anodic reactions, which has resulted in corrosion inhibition. The cause of the reduced rate of corrosion reactions can be found in the FTIR results of PF extract [15]. PF extract and SDS contain nitrogen, which, by sharing its electron pair, creates a bond between the extract and the electrode and consequently causes improved adsorption, which in turn leads to reduced corrosion rate. The results presented in Table 2 show that the inhibition effect of PF extract and SDS increases with the increase in the concentration of PF. It should be noted raising the concentration of SDS above 400 mg/L decreases the inhibition efficiency of this compound. As shown in Table 2, adding PF extract to 1 M HCl has decreased the corrosion current density (icorr), which means PF extract acts as a corrosion inhibitor for St37 steel in this medium. Table 2 also shows that in the presence of different concentrations of PF extract, the greatest change in corrosion potential is 26 mV. Another important parameter of Tafel polarization analysis is the slope of anodic and cathodic branches. In Fig. 2, it can be seen that there are only slight changes in the slopes of Tafel branches and these branches are largely parallel. From this, it can be concluded that the presence of different concentrations of the inhibitor has not made any significant change in the anodic and cathodic slopes of Tafel diagrams. This trend can also be observed in the results of Tables 2 and 3 .

The change in the corrosion potential in the presence of an inhibitor is an important determinant of the type of that inhibitor. The kinetic data contained in the potentiodynamic diagrams of this study showed that the change in the corrosion potential remained below 85 mV. Inhibitors that change the corrosion potential by less than 85 mV fall in the category of complex inhibitors [24]. Therefore, it can be stated that the inhibition effect of PF extract is of mixed type.

The presence of PF extract and SDS changes neither anodic reactions (metal dissolution) nor cathodic reactions (hydrogen reduction) and only affects the mechanism of charge and electron transfer [25]. Initially, PF and SDS molecules are adsorbed on the metal surface, thus blocking the active sites available on this surface. This decreases the number of sites available for the reduction of hydrogen cations, which in turn reduces the rate of hydrogen reduction reaction [26]. As the concentration of PF extract in the solution increases, it covers a larger portion of the surface, which increases the extent of corrosion inhibition.

In Table 2, it can be seen that the inhibition efficiency of PF extract for St37 steel in 1 M HCl reached a maximum of 70% at a concentration of 800 mg/L. For SDS, the highest inhibition efficiency was 56%, which was observed at a concentration of 400 mg/L. Here, the parameter θ with the following formulation is used to represent the extent of coverage of the surface with PF extract and SDS:
(3)
θ =
i 0
−
i
i n h .
i 0
In this equation, θ is the degree of surface coverage by the inhibitory film, which is used as a measure of the inhibitor's performance.

The changes in corrosion inhibition efficiency of PF extract and SDS in terms of inhibitor concentration are plotted in Fig. 3 . As clearly shown in Fig. 3, the inhibition efficiency of PF extract has increased with its concentration. For SDS, inhibition efficiency has initially increased with the concentration, but this trend has been reversed in concentrations of more than 400 mg/L. This is because as the concentration of SDS in the electrolyte rises above this threshold, SDS starts to coalesce into micelles that cannot efficiently participate in inhibition, and this undermines the resistance against aggressive ions, thereby reducing the corrosion inhibition of the metal surface [3]. This threshold concentration is called the critical micelle concentration (CMC). It should be noted that this study did not fully investigate the CMC of SDS. However, a study by Hosseini [3] has reported that the CMC of SDBS is about 279 ppm. This difference between the CMCs of SDS and SDBS is probably due to the presence of excess benzene rings in SDBS [3]. Research has shown that when an inhibitor gets adsorbed on a metal surface, the corrosion potential shifts to more negative values [27]. Table 3 shows that adding SDS to the electrolyte has increased the surface coverage (θ)from −0.24 to 0.56, which indicates the adsorption of SDS on the steel surface. However, at SDS concentration of 800 mg/L, the surface coverage (θ)has bounced back to 0.44, which means there has been a decrease in SDS adsorption on the steel surface [28]. This change can be attributed to micelle formation at concentrations higher than the CMC.

It should be noted that some compounds exhibit the opposite behavior at concentrations greater than the CMC. One group of these compounds is n-alkyl ammonium salts, which at concentrations above the CMC, coalesce into a coherent and continuous layer on the surface that protects is against the penetration of aggressive ions in the electrolyte [29, 30].

As shown in Fig. 3, the highest corrosion inhibition efficiency of SDS was observed at the concentration of 400 mg/L. Therefore, for the electrochemical impedance test, the concentration of SDS was fixed at 400 mg/L and the concentration of PF extract was changed to the levels listed in Table 4 . Fig. 4 displays the Nyquist plot and fitted data of the electrochemical impedance test of St37 steel in 1 M HCl medium in the presence of different concentrations of PF extract and 400 mg/L of SDS. The Nyquist curve in Fig. 4 can be divided into two regions. The first region is located at high and medium frequencies and shows a single, capacitive-like depressed semicircle. Due to the roughness and inhomogeneity of the electrode surface, these Nyquist curves are not perfect semicircles. In this case, instead of using the capacitor element in the model, a constant phase element (CPE) is used [31, 32]. The second region is located in the low-frequency range. At low frequencies, the Nyquist curves are elongated. This phenomenon has also been reported in some studies [33, 34]. The elongations in the Nyquist diagrams in the second region were attributed to accumulated molecules. At high concentrations of inhibitor, molecules cannot approach the metal surface and accumulate in the OHP (outer Helmholtz plane) side of the solution causing the elongation of a diffuse layer [33]. Previously, the same result (the presence of constant phase element (CPE) loop) had also been observed for the St37 steel immersed in 1 M HCl containing different concentrations of PF extract without SDS [15]. As the PF concentration was increased at a constant SDS concentration, the CPE loops of the Nyquist curve grew in diameter, which is a sign of the thickening of the film formed by the added compound on the metal surface [35]. In the next step, an equivalent circuit model with the polarization resistance placed parallel to the (CPE) was used to gain better insight into the function of the inhibitor on the metal surface. In this analysis, the capacitance generated by the inhibitor at the metal surface is described by a constant phase element (CPE) instead of the capacitor (C). The reason behind using CPE is the non-uniformity of the capacitive layer form on the surface. The impedance of CPE is given by Equation 4:
(4)
Z
C P E
=
1
Y 0
(
j w
)
n
where Z is the constant phase frequency,
Y 0
is the admittance, w is the angular frequency, j is a complex number, and n is a number between −1 and 1. The value of n decides the system condition: the system is considered a pure capacitor if n = 1, a pure resistor if n = 0, or an inductor if n=−1.

Table 4 presents the parameters extracted from the EIS test. In this table, it can be seen that as the concentration of PF extract in the electrolyte increases, the generated capacitance decreases and the resistance Rct increases. The reduced capacitance indicates that the inhibitor has been adsorbed on the metal-electrolyte interface, thus reducing the dielectric constant of the capacitor or in other words reinforcing the film formed by the PF extract on the metal surface [32, 36].

According to Eq. (5), which computes the capacitance generated in the dual-layer [8], as the capacitance decreases, the film thickness increases and the area of the electrode that is exposed to the corrosive electrolyte gets smaller:
(5)
C
d l
=
ε
0
ε
d
S

In this equation, Cdl is the capacitance generated in the dual-layer,
ε
0
is the dielectric constant of air,
ε
is the dielectric constant of the film, d is the thickness of the dual-layer, and S is the area of the electrode that is locally exposed to the electrolyte. According to the above equation, as the capacitance of the double layer decreases, firstly, the thickness of the film formed by the added compound (PF + SDS) increases, and secondly, the area of the electrode that is exposed to the corrosive electrolyte decreases. Therefore, the decrease in capacitance following the addition of the inhibitor to the electrolyte signifies the inhibitory function of this compound in 1 M HCl [37]. This shows that the PF extract has been indeed adsorbed on the metal-electrolyte interface and performed its inhibitory function.

Next, the Bode plots of the system were examined. As shown in Fig. 4-B, the polarization resistance has increased with the concentration of PF extract, which indicates that PF extract retains its inhibitory function when used with SDS. An increase in the impedance modulus (Z) is indicative of increased corrosion inhibition and decreased electrode corrosion rate [38-40].In Fig. 4-B, it can be seen that Z value has increased with the increase in the concentration of PF extract in the presence of SDS. From this, it can be concluded that in the presence of SDS, PF extract has shown higher inhibitory performance at higher concentrations.

Fig. 4-C shows the phase diagram of the system. According to this diagram, the presence of PF extract alongside SDS has shifted the peak of the curve toward higher values. It is also evident that in the presence of SDS, as the concentration of PF extract increases, the peak of this curve gets higher. This indicates that adding different concentrations of PF extract along with SDS to HCl decreases the rate of corrosion of the working electrode in this electrolyte [38].

The synergetic effect of SDS and PF extract was investigated by a potentiodynamic polarization test. For this purpose, polarization tests were performed at specific concentrations of SDS and PF extract at ambient temperature. The results of these tests are presented in Fig. 5 .

As illustrated in Fig. 5, the addition of these compounds to the tested electrolyte has not significantly changed the corrosion potential of the specimens. This figure also shows that with the addition of more PF and SDS, the vertical position of Tafel branches has shifted toward lower values, which shows the inhibition effect of these two compounds when used together. Another important point is the absence of any change in the slope of Tafel branches (see Table 5 ), which as explained in the previous sections, means there has been no change in the mechanism of cathodic and anodic reactions. In other words, the combination of PF and SDS has created an inhibition effect that reduces the rate of corrosion without altering the mechanisms of anodic and cathodic reactions. It can be claimed that the introduced inhibitor (PF + SDS) slows down both anodic and cathodic reactions [25].

As shown in Table 5, there has been no significant change in the corrosion potential. Since this potential only affects the thermodynamics of the corrosion reactions, it can be claimed that the addition of this extract and surfactant has not significantly altered the thermodynamics of the corrosion reactions.

In Table 5, it can be seen that as the concentration of PF and SDS in the electrolyte has increased, the current density has decreased and the electrolyte corrosion inhibition has improved. In other words, with the increase in the concentration of SDS and PF, the corrosion rate of the specimen inside 1 M HCl electrolyte has decreased. The decrease in the corrosion current is indicative of decreased hydrogen reduction in this system, which has resulted in a lower corrosion rate [41].

While the corrosion inhibition in the electrolyte containing only 100 mg/L of PF was approximately 44%, in the electrolyte containing 100 mg/L of PF plus 100 mg/L of SDS, the corrosion inhibition was about 31%. This shows that at these concentrations, PF and SDS have an antagonistic effect rather than synergetic. This was also the case for the PF concentration of 200 mg/L. In other words, to have a synergetic effect between PF and SDS, the concentration of PF should be greater than 200 mg/L. Eq. (6) was used to quantify the synergetic effect:
(6)
S =
1 −
η
P F
−
η
S D S
+
η
P F
η
S D S
1 −
η
P F . S D S

In this equation,
η
P F
is the efficiency of PF extract alone,
η
P F . S D S
is the efficiency PF with SDS, and S is the synergy factor. S > 1 reflects that the relation is synergetic, S < 1 shows that the relationship is antagonistic, S = 1 means that there is no synergetic or antagonistic relation between the compounds [3].
The S value of 1.32 in the bottom row of the table indicates that SDS and PF had a significant synergetic effect at the concentrations of PF = 800 mg/L and SDS = 400 mg/L.
As shown in Table 5, there is some degree of antagonistic effect at low concentrations of SDS and PF, which can be attributed to incomplete film formation [3].

In a study carried out by Villamil et al., it was reported that inside sulfuric acid, SDS got adsorbed on the copper electrode not only through the polar portion but also through the nonpolar portion [42]. This suggests that some of the electrical charges carried by the SDS are present not only in the inner Helmholtz layer but also in the outer layers, and this increases the electrostatic attraction between the oppositely charged ions, which improves the system adsorption [3, 42] and consequently the inhibition effect of PF in the presence of SDS. Also, the use of PF in the presence of SDS has improved the inhibitory function, which can be attributed to changes in the hemi-micellar structure. The presence of PF alters the hemi-micellar structure, which ultimately improves the inhibition effect of SDS [3].

AFM is one of the most powerful methods for studying surface roughness at the nanoscale, which is extremely useful for research on surface corrosion [43-45]. Fig. 6 displays the results of AFM analysis of the specimens after immersion in the corrosive electrolyte for 8 h in the presence of different concentrations of inhibitor.

As shown in Fig. 6, the presence of PF alongside 400 mg/L SDS has decreased the surface roughness due to corrosion. This demonstrates the inhibition performance of these two compounds when they are used together. It can be seen that as the PF level increases, the surface roughness decreases and predominately smoother grooves appear on the surface. The mean surface roughness values (Ra) obtained for the specimens are given in Fig. 7 . The surface with lowest average surface roughness (the smoothest surface) has been obtained in the presence of 800 mg/L PF and 400 mg/L SDS, which can be attributed to the reduction of the corrosion reactions or in other words lower rate of corrosion on the surface.

One way to study the corrosion inhibition effect of a substance inside an electrolyte is to compare the surfaces of the specimens immersed in that electrolyte with and without that substance [46]. Fig. 8 displays the FESEM images of the test specimens after immersion in the electrolyte containing different amounts of PF and SDS. As shown in Fig. 8, the presence of different concentrations of PF along with 400 mg/L of SDS has improved the corrosion inhibition performance of the system. The surface of the specimen in Fig. 8-a (400 mg/L SDS) shows more corrosion than others. With the increase in the concentration of PF, the final surface of the specimens has become smoother and more homogeneous, which means it has received better protection against aggressive ions (here Cl−). It is evident that when used in sufficient concentrations, PF and SDS create a layer of protective film on the metal surface which reduces the rate of corrosion damage. Naturally, greater smoothness of these surfaces reflects the stronger inhibition effect of the compound added to the electrolyte [46, 47].

The adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the metal surface exposed to corrosion can be analyzed by investigating the adsorption isotherms. The adsorption isotherm analysis is carried out with the help of parameter θ, which determines the degree of surface coverage by the corrosion inhibitor. According to Table 2, as the concentration of PF increases, so does θ. This indicates better adsorption of PF because higher θ signifies higher surface coverage by the inhibitor [48]. In the case of SDS, the results presented in Table 3 show when the concentration of SDS goes above 400 mg/L, θ starts to decrease, which indicates that at these concentrations (>400 mg/L), it is more difficult for SDS to get adsorbed on the surface. There are several mechanisms for analyzing adsorption isotherms, the simplest of which is Langmuir isotherm. Langmuir's model assumes that all sites on the metal surface are equal and the adsorption at each site is independent of the surrounding sites [32]. The relationship between the surface coverage (θ) and the concentration of the inhibitor in the electrolyte was examined using the Langmuir adsorption isotherm equation:
(7)
C θ
=
1 K
+ C
where K is the adsorption constant, θ is a factor of inhibition efficiency, which is calculated based on the corrosion current obtained from the Tafel test, and C is the concentration of the inhibitor in the electrolyte. Fig. 9 displays the plot of
C
/
θ
versus the concentration of PF and SDS according to the results of the polarization test at ambient temperature. As can be seen, the curve plotted in this figure is linear, which means
C
/
θ
changes linearly with the concentration of PF and SDS. This linearity reflects the simple adsorption of inhibitor particles on the metal-electrolyte interface [49]. From this, it can be concluded that the adsorption results obtained for PF and SDS are highly consistent with Langmuir isotherm conditions.

The thermodynamic parameters of the inhibitor adsorption were obtained from the van't Hoff equation [48]:
(8)
k =
1
55 / 5
exp
(
−
Δ
G
a d s
0
R T
)

Eqs. (7) and 8 were used to calculate the thermodynamic parameters of adsorption for PS and SDS. Using these equations, ∆Gads for PF at the concentration of 800 mg/L was calculated to −12.620 kJ. This is while ∆Gads for SDS at the concentrations of 400 mg/L and 800 mg/L were −12.840 kJ and −9.90 kJ, respectively. These figures suggest that from a thermodynamic point of view, the adsorption on the steel surface of SDS at a concentration of 400 mg/L is more spontaneous than at a concentration of 800 mg/L. At SDS concentration of 800 mg/L, the change in the free energy of adsorption is more positive than at the other concentration (400 mg/L). It can, therefore, be concluded there is more potential for the adsorption of SDS at 400 mg/L, which is consistent with the results of electrochemical tests [48].

The inhibition effect of SDS and PF on the surface of St37 steel can also be analyzed in terms of the stability of the film formed on the surface of the working electrode. The inhibitors that prevent corrosion reactions between metal and electrolyte increase the activation energy of the metal dissolution reaction. This change inhibits the dissolution of metal in the corrosive medium, as this dissolution would require significant amounts of activation energy [50].

The results of this study can be summarized as follows.
1
Because of the tannin contained in Prosopis farcta, the extract of this plant is a relatively good inhibitor for the corrosion of St37 steel in 1 M HCl at ambient temperature.
2
As the concentration of PF extract and SDS in the electrolyte (1 M HCl) increased, so did the resulting corrosion inhibition effect on St37 steel specimens. However, increasing the concentration of SDS above a critical threshold adversely affected the inhibition efficiency.
3
The results of the polarization test showed that the introduced inhibitor (PF+SDS) is a mixed type corrosion inhibitor and only slows down the corrosion rather than changing its mechanism.
4
The impedance test showed that charge and electron transfer resistance increased with the increasing concentration of the inhibitor.
5
According to Langmuir adsorption isotherm theory, the adsorption isotherm of the PF+SDS inhibitor is stable.
6
The results of the polarization test showed that the highest inhibition effect of the PF+SDS inhibitor for St37 steel in 1 M HCl at ambient temperature is 90%, which occurs at the concentrations of 400 mg/L SDS + 800 mg/L PF.
7
. It was found that the combination of 800 mg/L PF extract and 400 mg/L SDS have a synergistic effect.
8
By forming a layer of coating on the surface of the St37 electrode, SDS and PF protected this electrode against corrosion in 1 M HCl.
9
The presence of SDS and PF in the 1 M HCl electrolyte protected the specimen surface and reduced the surface roughness due to corrosion.

Morteza Ferdosi Heragh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Hossein Tavakoli: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fontana, M.J., Corrosion Engineering.2nd ed, Mac-Hill, New York, 1987.
